nameMeanings.name: Fitch
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: from Old French fiche, perhaps ‘pointed implement for fixing or transfixing something or someone’ (such as a lance?), a derivative of Old French fichier ‘to fix, fasten, pin on, stick into, pierce’. Compare Modern French fiche ‘peg, pin’. Reaney remarks that ‘as Hugh Malet is said to have abandoned for a time his nickname ‘little hammer’ in favor of Fichet (see Mallet ), fiche must have been used of a pointed weapon, a spear or lance, and Fitch and Fitchett (see Fitchett ) of a spearman or a knight famous for his exploits with the lance’. Use of Fiche as a personal name is possibly implied by diminutive personal name forms such as Fechet (see Fitchett ) and Fechel, attested in Fechel de Fercalahn, 1225–50. The latter is perhaps the source of the now extinct English surname Fetchell.

variants.name: Fitchett
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from Old French Fichet, Fichot, or (in northern France) Fiket, Fikot, diminutive forms of fiche or fike ‘pointed implement for fixing or transfixing something or someone’ (such as a spear?), used as a nickname and as a personal name. See Fitch .

variants.name: Mallet
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Jean-Yves, Adelard, Alain, Henri, Jacques, Leonce, Lucien, Monique, Philippe.

French: nickname from a diminutive of Mal ‘bad’. Alternatively, from a derivative of the related Old French word maleit ‘accursed’ (from Latin maledictus, the opposite of benedictus ‘blessed’), applied as a nickname for an unlucky person. Compare Malet 1.
French: variant of   Malet   2, an Old French pet form of the personal name   Malo  .

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

en.lifeSummary: When Robert Fitch was born in 1524, in Bocking, Essex, England, his father, Thomas Fytche, was 30 and his mother, Margaret Meade, was 32. He married Ellen Carver in 1549, in Essex, England. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 2 daughters. He died about 1588, in Essex, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 65, and was buried in Allesley, Warwickshire, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Robert Fitch est né en 1524, à Bocking, Essex, Angleterre, son père, Thomas Fytche, avait 30 ans et sa mère, Margaret Meade, avait 32 ans. Il a épousé Ellen Carver en 1549, à Essex, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 2 filles. Il est décédé environ 1588, à Essex, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 65 ans, et a été enterré à Allesley, Warwickshire, Angleterre.

ko.lifeSummary: Robert Fitch(이)가 1524년 영국 에세스 보킨그에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Thomas Fytche(은)는 30세였고, 그의 어머니 Margaret Meade(은)는 32세였다. 그는 1549년 영국 에세스에서 Ellen Carver(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 대략 1588년 영국 잉글랜드 에세스에서 65세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 워윅실르 앨레슬리에 묻혔다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Robert Fitch родился в 1524 г. в местности боккинг, эссекс, Англия, его отцу по имени Thomas Fytche было 30 лет, а матери, которую звали Margaret Meade, 32. Он женился на женщине по имени Ellen Carver в 1549 г. в местности эссекс, Англия. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 2 дочери. Он умер около 1588 г. в местности эссекс, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 65 лет и был похоронен в местности аллесли, варвиккшир, Англия.
